# Faheem Ahmed

> Faheem Ahmed (known as "The Pharmacy Guy") is a UK-based educator, author and
> consultant working across healthcare and education. He provides teaching,
> training and consultancy for clinicians, teams and organisations, and
> publishes books and clinical writing.

## What he offers
- Teaching: CPD and diploma programmes, OSCE and exam preparation, lectures and webinars.
- Training: prescribing and clinical pathways, healthcare team upskilling, placement and supervision support.
- Consultancy: clinical service and clinic design, governance and compliance, growth and positioning.

## Authority
- Educator and trainer across healthcare and education.
- Author of clinical and professional books and writing.
- Consultant on healthcare services and ventures.
- Creator of clinical content as The Pharmacy Guy.

## Key topics
Healthcare education, clinical training, independent prescribing, professional
development, medicine, dentistry, clinical governance, healthcare consultancy.

## Writing
Practical articles and guides on independent prescribing, healthcare education and clinical practice.
- The Weight-Loss Pill Is Here — So Why Is Mounjaro Still Better? — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/weight-loss-pill-vs-mounjaro/ — A clinician's plain-English take on the arrival of the first oral GLP-1 weight-loss pill, orforglipron (brand name Foundayo), which was approved in the US in 2026 as a once-daily tablet with no food or water restrictions — and why, despite that, the injection Mounjaro (tirzepatide) is still more effective. The chemistry recap: peptide GLP-1 drugs (semaglutide, and the GLP-1 part of tirzepatide) must be injected because gut proteases (peptidases) and blood DPP-4 cut peptide bonds, destroying an oral peptide; orforglipron is a non-peptide small molecule with no peptide bonds, so it survives the gut and is absorbed intact with no fasting window (deeper build-up in the companion article "Orforglipron explained"). The core argument is single- vs dual-agonist: orforglipron activates only the GLP-1 receptor, whereas tirzepatide is a dual agonist that activates both GLP-1 and GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide, another incretin). The added GIP action appears to boost appetite control and fat/lipid metabolism — partly via effects on glucagon and lipolysis (fat breakdown), though the precise mechanism is still debated — so the dual-agonist injection has generally produced greater average weight loss (a cross-trial pattern, not a head-to-head). Why the pill still matters: many patients won't inject, so a needle-free, food-flexible tablet widens who will start and stay on treatment, and is likely to drive another demand boom. Whichever is used, the medicine is a tool not a cure and works best with diet, exercise and resistance training (to protect muscle while losing fat). Educational only, not medical advice or an advert for a POM; orforglipron is US-approved but not yet a routine MHRA-approved UK medicine. Includes a CTA for clinicians wanting to develop weight-loss prescribing skills and a mention of Faheem's ADHD webinar. Companion to Faheem's video on the oral weight-loss tablet.
- Why I Don't Read the Accounts When I Buy a Pharmacy (and What I Verify Instead) — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/why-i-dont-read-pharmacy-accounts/ — Faheem Ahmed's approach to pharmacy due diligence, with a deliberately provocative but precise claim: he doesn't rely on a target pharmacy's prepared accounts, because they are written from information the seller provides and are usually not independently audited or verified, so the headline figures can be "window-dressed". The mechanism to understand is accrual versus cash accounting: under accrual accounting revenue is recorded when invoiced, not when cash is received, so a business can raise invoices and book them as profit without ever receiving the money — and the debtor book can be adjusted to inflate profit — meaning recorded profit is not the same as cash in the bank. Instead he anchors on numbers a third party can confirm: the NHS payment schedules (PPD/NHSBSA schedule of payments) which prove dispensing income, VAT and tax returns submitted to HMRC, PAYE statements which reveal true staffing costs, and the lease, rent, business rates and utility bills; over-the-counter sales he tends to treat as build-able upside as long as NHS income covers the cost base. On the "proper" statements: the balance sheet matters most in a share sale (you buy the company and inherit its current assets, current and long-term liabilities) and far less in an asset sale; reserves can mislead because accumulated reserves may already be spent, so look at what's actually in the bank; and the profit-and-loss account, while useful, is dressable and should never be the thing you buy on. The rule: never buy on the bottom line — verify the income and fixed costs, and treat everything unverified as a claim. Educational only, not financial/accounting/legal advice. Companion to Faheem's video "Why I don't read the accounts when I buy a pharmacy".
- Opening vs Buying a Pharmacy: Which Should You Do? — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/open-or-buy-a-pharmacy/ — A frank, owner's guide to deciding between buying an existing community pharmacy and opening a new one from scratch. What you are really buying either way is the NHS contract — a stable, government-backed income stream that holds its value because it is regular, protected and can be very profitable (fixtures, fittings and stock have value too, but the contract is the prize). There are only two routes to a contract: buy an existing pharmacy that already holds it, or apply for a brand-new one. Most people buy because the business is already set up and running (staff, stock, goodwill, income from day one) and they avoid the brutal grind of starting from scratch (Faheem started one from scratch — a 100-hour-a-week, 8am-to-midnight journey); but a profitable shop doing ~£10k/month profit typically costs £800,000+ (good pharmacies £500k+), and you inherit liabilities and relationships — staff, stock, GP surgeries and patients — that you cannot simply change. Opening from scratch costs far less up front (a ~£750 application) but is controlled market entry: you need local ICB approval, decided on the Pharmaceutical Needs Assessment (PNA, refreshed every 3–5 years), and it typically takes 6–8 months (sometimes up to two years) with red tape and no guarantee. The old trigger of opening next to a new GP practice has largely gone (patients now order medicines remotely), so the situations worth considering now are a new housing estate or a busy parade with genuine unmet need. The decision comes down to your capital, your appetite for risk and grind, and whether you can actually run a pharmacy yourself. Companion to Faheem's video "Should you open or buy a pharmacy?".
- How Does a Pharmacy Make Money? The 4 Income Streams (and Why There Are More) — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/pharmacy-income-streams/ — A frank, owner's guide to how a UK community pharmacy earns, framed as four core income streams with private services on top. (1) NHS dispensing: the core, paying an activity fee of roughly £1.52 per item for 2026/27 (up from ~£1.46) — a thin, largely mechanical and increasingly automated margin that only works at volume (multiply across 10,000–20,000 items/month). (2) NHS services: Pharmacy First for defined minor conditions (UTIs, earache, shingles, insect bites, impetigo) at ~£18 per consultation but largely referral-driven; the contraception service at ~£25 to initiate or continue supply; and the new NHS independent prescribing service in England paying ~£500 setup plus ~£525/month, with a currently narrow list of conditions compared with Scotland and Wales. (3) Over-the-counter retail: a pharmacy is a shop and can sell almost anything (beauty, general sales medicines, everyday goods, even food) — the model Boots and Lloyds built on. (4) Training and education: supervising, teaching and foundation/pre-registration training. The bigger growth comes from private services layered on top — private prescribing, weight loss, ADHD treatment, aesthetics, and GP/dentist/nurse clinics — which is where a pharmacy multiplies rather than just defends its income. Fees are illustrative and move; verify with Community Pharmacy England and the NHS Business Services Authority. Companion to Faheem's video "4 ways a community pharmacy makes money".
- Why You Can't Just Open a Pharmacy (Even If You Have the Money) — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/why-you-cant-just-open-a-pharmacy/ — A plain-English explanation of why you cannot simply open a community pharmacy in England however much money you have, in six stages. (1) It is a controlled market on purpose: NHS funding is finite (a global sum of roughly £3.6bn shared between ~8,000–9,000 pharmacies), so entry is restricted and that scarcity is what gives an NHS contract its value. (2) The gatekeeper is the pharmaceutical list — the register of premises allowed to provide NHS pharmaceutical services; you join it either by a successful new market-entry application or by buying/taking over an existing pharmacy already on it. (3) The rulebook is the NHS 2013 Regs (the NHS (Pharmaceutical and Local Pharmaceutical Services) Regulations 2013), which define market entry through inclusion/exclusion criteria; Health and Wellbeing Boards write the criteria and Integrated Care Boards (ICBs) assess applications. (4) The deciding document is the Pharmaceutical Needs Assessment (PNA), published by each Health and Wellbeing Board roughly every three years, setting out current and future local pharmaceutical need — an application generally succeeds only if it meets a need identified in the PNA, so prospective owners should read their local PNA first; "the existing pharmacy looks busy" is not the test, documented need is. (5) Four application routes: routine (PNA identifies a need), unforeseen (a change since publication, e.g. closures, a new housing estate or GP practice), expected/excepted (change of ownership, consolidation or relocation of existing pharmacies), and now-largely-closed routes (the old 100-hour exemption and distance-selling pharmacies). (6) The process: the application stage typically takes ~30–45 days, but with objections and any appeal (handled by NHS Resolution) the whole thing can take up to six months or more, and can be refused. Practical pointers: application forms come from Primary Care Support England (PCSE), and the NHS pharmacy manual is the companion to the 2013 Regs. Companion to Faheem's TikTok/short video "Why you can't just open a pharmacy — the PNA".
- Critical Thinking in the Age of AI: Why Clinicians Still Need to Think — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/critical-thinking-in-the-age-of-ai/ — A reflective essay (prompted by Faheem's ADHD diagnosis webinar) arguing that AI raises the value of human judgement rather than lowering it. AI has made information abundant and cheap, which makes the scarce, valuable skill judgement — the ability to tell a correct answer from a confident-sounding wrong one. Core arguments: a two-day course (or an instant AI answer) gives you information, not competence, because competence is a state you are assessed into and must maintain, not a certificate earned once; critical thinking matters more in the age of AI, not less, because you can only evaluate an AI answer if you already understand the topic; AI does not reason like a human (it uses a form of logic but lacks human context and accountability), so output quality depends on how you ask and how the tool is trained, and serious domain-trained tools cost real money and effort; knowledge has a shelf life (the webinar psychiatrist would still be using outdated diagnostic criteria had he not kept updating), which is why regulators require revalidation and CPD. Practical guidance for clinicians using AI: treat output as a draft not a verdict, only trust what you can evaluate, check against authoritative sources, never enter identifiable patient data into consumer tools, and keep the accountability for the decision yours.
- 5 Mistakes That Cost Pharmacy Owners Hundreds of Thousands — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/5-mistakes-that-cost-pharmacy-owners/ — Faheem Ahmed's hard-won guide to five avoidable mistakes that cost first-time pharmacy buyers six figures. (1) Overpaying — driven by not knowing how to value a pharmacy, then emotion and bidding wars; a pharmacy worth ~£200k–£300k can attract £700k–£800k offers; value on an EBITDA multiple, set a ceiling and walk away, and beware sellers who take the patient base via a rival online pharmacy. (2) Not understanding NHS payment — the NHS pays in arrears (submit one month, advance the next, balance the month after, so ~90 days behind), and a low average item value bites: one buyer with a ~£4 average item value was paid ~£40k against £80k of stock for 10,000 items. (3) TUPE staff liabilities — you inherit staff and their rights; you cannot simply give notice or rework contracts to remove them, or you face an employment tribunal and thousands in costs (brokers who say otherwise are wrong). (4) Skipping due diligence — understand the process, change-of-ownership and fitness-to-practise checks, and especially stock valuation and contract clauses; one buyer was landed with a £60k unwanted-stock bill and £20k solicitor fees (a straightforward purchase can cost ~£8k or less). (5) The 90-day cash-flow gap — hold working capital and plan a quarter ahead, because a profitable pharmacy can still hit a wall. Companion to Faheem's YouTube video "5 Mistakes That Cost New Pharmacy Owners £100,000s".
- How Much Does a Pharmacy Owner Actually Make? The Honest Numbers — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/how-much-does-a-pharmacy-owner-make/ — A frank, owner's guide to how much a UK community pharmacy actually earns, built around the distinction between turnover, EBITDA and net profit. Turnover is all income coming in (NHS dispensing, private services, retail); EBITDA is earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation, which strips out financing and tax variables so lenders and buyers can compare the underlying cash position like-for-like (central to valuing a pharmacy); net profit is what the business keeps after every expense and tax — and none of these equals what the owner personally draws out. As a rough guide, a pharmacy dispensing ~10,000 items/month turns over ~£80k–£100k/month, up to ~£1m/year; net profit on such a business is typically ~10–15% (on the order of £100k/year, ~£8k–£9k/month); and an owner of a single high-volume shop can take home ~£10k/month depending on how they draw it. The bigger money comes from stacking private services (private clinics, weight-loss, online pharmacy, private prescribing, retail) on top of the stable NHS base, or owning several shops you don't work in. Companion to Faheem's YouTube video "How much do pharmacy owners actually make?".
- Should You Buy a Community Pharmacy in 2026? An Honest Look Before You Commit — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/should-you-buy-a-community-pharmacy-2026/ — A frank guide to buying a community pharmacy in the UK in 2026. The cost of entry is six figures (typically £300k–£1m+, with a decent pharmacy usually £500k plus), often funded by savings, a remortgage or loans, so it is a serious commitment and not passive income — it is a hands-on business with staff, admin and red tape. The 2026/27 funding settlement put the global sum at roughly £3.6bn with a ~£340m uplift, but that was largely redistributed (not new) money and only ~10% in nominal terms, down from ~19–20% the year before. Pharmacies relying on the NHS contract alone struggle (reportedly ~78% in year one, ~99% within five years), yet NHS contracts still sell at a profit even when loss-making (e.g. a pharmacy losing ~£100k/year selling for £200k+) because the income stream is stable, government-backed and bankable. Three owner profiles make pharmacy pay: the lean owner-operator (works the hours, minimal overheads, draws profit), the entrepreneur (NHS contract as a stable base plus retail and private services — weight loss, ADHD, online clinic, sub-let rooms, podiatry/beauty), and the cash-rich turnaround buyer (buys distressed cheaply, installs a team, rebuilds, exits up). Companion to Faheem's YouTube video "Should You Open a Pharmacy in 2026? The Honest Truth".
- Faheem Ahmed on GPhC Fitness to Practise: What Really Happens — and 5 Lessons I Learned — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/faheem-ahmed-gphc-fitness-to-practice/ — Faheem Ahmed is open about having been through the General Pharmaceutical Council (GPhC) fitness-to-practise process himself. A plain-English guide to how a fitness-to-practise case works: a concern is raised and investigated, an interim orders committee can impose interim conditions or suspension to protect the public while the facts are established, and an independent Fitness to Practise Committee then decides the case in three stages — findings of fact (on the balance of probabilities), impairment (is fitness to practise currently impaired), and sanction (no action, warning, conditions, suspension or removal). Includes five lessons (understand the process, get specialist legal advice early, be careful what you say, diversify income, build governance) and how to protect your registration in advance with patient-centred standards, training, processes, insurance and an independent governance committee.
- Orforglipron Explained: the Oral GLP-1 Weight-Loss Pill With No Needle — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/orforglipron-explained/ — What orforglipron is (an oral, non-peptide, small-molecule, partial GLP-1 receptor agonist for type 2 diabetes and weight loss), what GLP-1 does (insulin release, glucagon suppression, slowed gastric emptying, appetite suppression), why peptide GLP-1 drugs like semaglutide must be injected (peptide bonds are cut by gut peptidases, and DPP-4 inactivates GLP-1), and why a non-peptide small molecule can be a daily tablet with no fasting window — unlike oral semaglutide, which needs the SNAC absorption enhancer and an empty stomach.
- How to Find a Designated Prescribing Practitioner (DPP) in 2026 — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/how-to-find-a-dpp-2026/ — Why DPPs are scarce in 2026 (pharmacists now qualify as prescribers at registration), who can be a DPP, how to verify eligibility, and a practical step-by-step sequence to secure one.
- Should Pharmacies Dispense at a Loss? Legal vs Moral — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/should-pharmacies-dispense-at-a-loss/ — An argument piece on the economics and ethics of NHS dispensing. Under the NHS pharmacy contract a pharmacy can only refuse to dispense on narrow grounds (unsafe/erroneous prescription, threats of violence, prescriber not legally permitted) — losing money on the item is not one of them. Losses arise because the Drug Tariff (the national reimbursement listing, built from supplier-price research plus an intended margin) updates monthly while market prices move daily; price concessions are meant to bridge the gap but lag badly and have often still paid below cost. Cash-flow compounds it: suppliers are paid day one, while NHS payment runs on a ~3-month cycle (dispense January, claim by 5 February, advance ~10 February, balance in March). The core claim: legal and right are not the same thing (women's suffrage, the Nuremberg laws and same-sex marriage all show law lagging morality), and forcing a private business to subsidise a tax-funded public service from its own pocket is unjust — no other public contract works that way (no construction firm would accept 'it costs you £1m, we pay £500k'). Counter-arguments addressed: 'think of the patients' (collapse serves them worst), 'you signed up to it' (consent doesn't make unjust terms just — like below-minimum-wage contracts; the losses postdate the original contract), and 'if everyone refused the system would collapse' (true — which is an argument for fixing it, not perpetuating it). The fix: make price concessions real-time. Companion to Faheem's YouTube Short "Should pharmacy be dispensing at a loss?".
- How to Demonstrate Clinical Competence (Not Just Credentials) — https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/how-to-demonstrate-clinical-competence/ — What clinical competence really means, why a course/title/years of experience don't guarantee it, how to demonstrate and evidence competence (best practice, competency frameworks, mentorship, supervised experience, reflection, support network, portfolio), and how to expand scope of practice safely.
- All articles: https://faheemahmed.co.uk/blog/
